However, with regulators keeping a close watch on foreign ownership and market stability, any deal will likely face rigorous scrutiny before it can be finalised.

data.title: Clawdbot author: Billionaire Benhao's comeback is only due to the emptiness of retirement.
data.description:
The creator of Clawdbot, which has gone viral online, is actually a billionaire?!

I initially thought he was just another AI entrepreneur who dropped out of school, but after checking online, I found out that this guy started a business 15 years ago and is probably forty years old this year.

And his first venture took off immediately.

Started in 2011, cashed out and exited in 2021, making a hefty profit of100 million euros(approximately 830 million RMB). Logically, the script for achieving financial freedom should be: golf by day, wine and lobster by night. But guess what? After only four years of leisurely living, this guy couldn't sit still anymore. His hands were itching to get back to coding full-time, venturing into AI entrepreneurship. In less than a year, he created Clawdbot. A 24/7 open-source AI assistant that proactively reports to users like a personal secretary. This agent can do everything a human can—fill out forms, send emails, control browsers… a true "Jarvis." This phenomenal AI product, which went viral online, also helped Tim Cook boost Mac mini sales.

Netizens shouted: Absolute GOAT. Let's take a look at the legendary life of Clawdbot's creator, the "father of Jarvis". First Half: The Self-Made iOS Engineer The Boss is Peter Steinberger (let's call him Peter from now on). He's Austrian and graduated from the Vienna University of Technology with a degree in Computer and Information Science. In fact, even while still a student, Peter was already a seasoned iOS developer. During his studies, he provided technical consulting and one-on-one training for several well-known iOS projects. Even the first Mac/iOS development course at his alma mater was launched with Peter's involvement and promotion. In 2010, Peter received an order that would change his life thanks to his skills. The client wanted to create a magazine-style app based on PDF format. Peter spent eight weeks developing a complete iOS version for them. Initially, he didn't think much of it. But not long after, a colleague suddenly asked him, "Can I use this engine too?" Peter realized this was no small matter. At that time, the first-generation iPad had just been released, and applications were increasingly shifting experiences to the digital world. Paper documents were likely among the first to be restructured. Once documents were digitized, additional features such as electronic signatures, document viewing, editing, annotation, and collaboration needed to be added. Companies wanting to do these things themselves faced a high technical barrier. Therefore, in 2011, Peter launched a Solo project—PSPDFKit. The name is also very straightforward: P comes from Peter; PDF refers to PDF files; Kit represents the SDK toolkit. The project progressed exceptionally quickly. Peter built a website with an online store almost in a day and posted a message on Twitter. Several licenses were sold in the first week. However, at that time he didn't intend to start a business full-time. In fact, Peter had already accepted a programmer job in Silicon Valley. It was just that the work visa processing for the United States was exceptionally slow, taking a full nine months. But it was precisely these nine months that gave him an unexpected "window of opportunity." He didn't take on any other part-time jobs and devoted all his time to polishing PSPDFKit. Who could have imagined that this side hustle would progress so smoothly…? It was profitable almost from day one. By the time Peter officially joined the Silicon Valley company, the revenue generated by PSPDFKit had already exceeded his salary. Four months later, Peter returned to his hometown, going all-in on PSPDFKit, officially expanding the team, and bringing in two co-founders. As it turns out, he truly caught the wave: document processing tools, even those with features like "previewing cloud files," were incredibly difficult to implement at the time, yet essential for businesses. Now, PSPDFKit has laid the foundation, allowing businesses to directly integrate the SDK and API for a subscription fee. Peter's business naturally boomed. By 2021, PSPDFKit had become the de facto standard in the document processing field. Clients include well-known companies such as Apple, Adobe, Lufthansa, Dropbox, and Disney, indirectly serving nearly 1 billion consumers in 150 countries. Peter's life also took a turn that year. In October 2021, the renowned firm Insight Partners announced an investment in PSPDFKit. Peter chose this juncture to cash out and exit, selling most of his shares for approximately €100 million. Peter's Second Half But the story is only just beginning... Having achieved financial freedom, Peter felt an unprecedented emptiness. When I sold my shares in PSPDFKit, my heart was breaking. PSPDFKit was Peter's 13 years of hard work. But he was simply too tired and had to take a break, and the emergence of Insight Partners provided a valuable opportunity. After retiring, Peter indulged in revelry and pleasure, even moving to another country. But all of this only made him feel like a walking corpse. To fill the emptiness inside, he tried various forms of psychotherapy and even daphne—a traditional hallucinogenic drink originating from South America. Four years passed in this way. Until one day, Peter suddenly realized: moving cities and countries couldn't bring true happiness; the meaning of life might never be found. Because the true meaning—needs me to create it myself. One day, Peter suddenly had a new idea, and sat down at the computer to start writing code. At that moment, I realized that my spark had returned. He suddenly understood that putting his ideas into practice and writing code had always been the happiest thing in his life. So, in June 2025, Peter announced his comeback on his blog. On LinkedIn, his latest identity is the founder of Amantus Machina, dedicated to developing next-generation hyper-personalized AI agents. Six months later, Clawbot became a viral sensation online, becoming the first truly viral AI product of 2026. While listening to a podcast the other day, the founder of Lovart mentioned a point: In the mobile internet era, the head effect was too strong. Many excellent entrepreneurs of the previous generation didn't even have a chance to fully demonstrate their abilities before the window of opportunity closed. Many people are eager to try. But now, technology is tearing open an unprecedentedly huge window. The emergence of AI has finally given us the opportunity to completely remake all products. For many entrepreneurs of the previous generation, this was like a ticket that had been long overdue. Peter was thinking the same thing: We're back… it's time to make a big splash.

data.title: Japan Targets 2028 for Crypto ETFs as Regulators Weigh Investor Protections
data.description:
Why 2028 Is Emerging as the Target

Japan’s financial regulators are laying the groundwork for a major shift in crypto market access, with discussions underway that could allow spot cryptocurrency exchange-traded funds (ETFs) to launch as early as 2028, according to multiple reports citing people familiar with the matter.

Japan is jumping on the crypto ETF bandwagon🇯🇵

Japan is eyeing to approve its first crypto ETFs as early as 2028, with Nomura and SBI Holdings expected to launch the first lineup of funds.

This comes after US BTC ETFs accumulated $115B+ in net assets, and Hong Kong became… pic.twitter.com/udZCgEaIA0

— Danny Kunwoong Park (@ParkKunwoong) January 26, 2026

At the center of the proposal is Japan’s Financial Services Agency (FSA), which is considering changes to its regulatory framework that would classify cryptocurrencies as eligible ETF assets for the first time. If implemented, the move would end Japan’s longstanding ban on spot crypto ETFs and mark a significant recalibration of the country’s cautious approach to digital assets.

Regulators’ focus on 2028 reflects the complexity of rewriting Japan’s ETF and investor-protection rules rather than an imminent approval, according to Nikkei. Any change would require formal consultations, revisions to existing asset classification rules, and safeguards tailored to retail investors.

Under current regulations, ETFs in Japan are restricted to traditional asset classes, keeping direct crypto exposure outside the framework. By contrast, allowing spot crypto ETFs would enable funds holding assets like Bitcoin and Ether to list on the Tokyo Stock Exchange, offering investors regulated access through standard brokerage accounts without the need for wallets or private key management.

--LAW DAY 33--

Under the new Japan Law set to take effect next year, only licensed entities such as fund transfer service providers or trust banks can issue or distribute fiat-pegged stablecoins, a framework that has opened the door to regulated launches like RLUSD. https://t.co/yd1bjCIqQipic.twitter.com/fzIU3tgNJj

— Chad Steingraber (@ChadSteingraber) August 22, 2025

Nikkei estimates that Japan’s crypto ETF market could eventually reach 1 trillion yen in assets, though the figure remains speculative and dependent on final regulatory decisions and market conditions.

The extended timeline also reflects lessons learned from overseas markets. The United States and Hong Kong approved their first spot crypto ETFs in 2024, providing regulators with real-world data on custody models, market surveillance, and investor behavior that Japan appears keen to study before moving forward.

Japan is inching toward its first spot crypto ETFs—expected by 2028. Yeah, the wait feels eternal, but over 60% of local investors are itching for crypto exposure. Big players like Nomura and SBI are already prepping spicy ETF combos (gold-Bitcoin, Bitcoin-XRP). Regulators remain… pic.twitter.com/q2SraJYnYs

— Fama Crypto (@Famacrypt) January 26, 2026

Institutions Prepare as Demand Builds

Despite regulatory hurdles, Japan’s largest financial institutions are already positioning themselves for a potential launch. Nomura Holdings and SBI Holdings are widely seen as early candidates to issue the country’s first crypto-linked ETFs once rules are finalized.

SBI has previously disclosed plans for multiple products, including a Bitcoin–XRP ETF and a gold-and-crypto hybrid fund, each contingent on regulatory approval. In August 2025, a Nomura executive cited survey data showing that more than 60% of Japanese investors want exposure to crypto assets in some form, underscoring pent-up demand.

The push from industry coincides with broader policy signals from Tokyo. In early January, Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama described 2026 as Japan’s “Digital Year One.”

"In the US, crypto assets are increasingly used via ETFs as inflation hedges, and Japan must also pursue advanced fintech initiative."

🚨BIG NEWS: 😳😳

SBI Holdings just applied to Japan’s FSA for TWO crypto ETFs featuring $XRP, Bitcoin, and gold-backed assets! 🇯🇵🔥

If approved, this would be Japan’s first regulated XRP ETF game changer for institutional adoption! 💥 pic.twitter.com/VrvC0DCWhW

— Xaif Crypto🇮🇳|🇺🇸 (@Xaif_Crypto) August 6, 2025

A Broader Shift in Japan’s Crypto Stance

While officials have stopped short of confirming a timeline, the ETF discussions form part of a wider reassessment of Japan’s digital asset policies. Proposed reforms under consideration include reducing crypto capital gains taxes, allowing banks and brokerages to hold digital assets, and reclassifying major cryptocurrencies as financial products.

🚨 🇯🇵 JAPAN SLASHES CRYPTO TAX FROM 55% TO 20%

I AM SPEECHLESS 😶👇 https://t.co/yFkFkK4QSOpic.twitter.com/QXfw8WetnE

— Pumpius (@pumpius) December 1, 2025

Approval of spot crypto ETFs would represent a clear break from Japan’s historically conservative posture and help the country keep pace with regional competitors. South Korea has signaled plans to introduce Bitcoin ETFs by 2026, increasing pressure on Japan to remain competitive as capital and innovation gravitate toward more accommodating jurisdictions.

For now, regulators emphasize that the 2028 target reflects intent rather than approval. But if realized, the framework could reshape how Japanese investors access crypto—moving digital assets further into the mainstream of the country’s financial system.
